questions.question: What is the primary benefit of 'Building in Public' (BIP) for a solo founder?

options.text: It hides your competitors from your progress.
It creates a 'Magnet Effect' by building trust through consistent transparency and documentary-style progress.
It allows you to get more ads for free.
It automatically generates a product roadmap for you.

questions.explanation: BIP allows potential customers to 'live the journey' with you, building a level of deep trust that traditional marketing cannot achieve.

questions.question: Which of these is considered a 'Strategic Transparency' rule?

options.text: Share every single line of code you write.
Post your personal bank balance daily.
Share trends and milestones (like MRR %) rather than sensitive competitive secrets or exact private data.
Never mention failures.

questions.explanation: Transparency is a tool for building trust, but it must be balanced with professional privacy and competitive intelligence safety.

questions.question: Why do 'Threads' typically outperform single posts on Twitter/X?

options.text: Because they are harder to read.
Because they allow for deeper storytelling, provide more value, and the algorithm favors them for retention.
Because you can link to more websites.
They don't; single posts are always better.

questions.explanation: Threads enable a founder to share a full narrative (Problem -> Story -> Lesson), which captures attention and provides more value than a single character-limited post.

questions.question: What is the 'Reply Strategy' mentioned in the lesson?

options.text: Automatically replying 'Great post!' to everyone.
Finding 20 'A-List' thinkers in your niche and contributing unique practitioner insights to their conversations daily.
DMing everyone who likes a post.
Replying to your own posts to make them look busy.

questions.explanation: Replies are the 'outer edge' of your acquisition engine. By being helpful in high-traffic threads, you earn visibility and authority without needing a huge initial following.

questions.question: Which type of BIP content is often the MOST engaging for the 'Tech Twitter' community?

options.text: A standard feature release announcement.
Sharing a 'Post-Mortem' of a failure or a hard lesson learned.
A generic motivational quote.
A link to your pricing page.

questions.explanation: Vulnerability is authentic. Sharing a failure and how you solved it proves you are a practitioner who deeply cares about the work and the users.

questions.question: Identify one 'Micro-Win' and one 'Mini-Fail' you've experienced in the last 14 days. If you were to share them as a single 'BIP Lesson' on Twitter, what would the title of that post be? (Focus on the transformation: e.g., 'From [Fail] to [Win] in 48 hours').
promptHints: Include a specific metric if possible (time saved, bugs fixed, etc.).
Explain the 'Human' feeling behind the event.
End with a 'Lesson for other founders'.
